3: > Founder-led, AI-native agency. Triadic collective of senior-only specialists.

5: 
6: ## 1. Operating model

15: CEO / Founder — Brahim Amir Zerbout
16: (AI developer, Linux/security, CRM architect; central operator & final sign-off)

18: ├── DISCIPLINE 1 — STRATEGIC INTELLIGENCE  (Head of Intelligence)
19: │   ├── Data Scientist          — predictive models, analytics pipelines

25: │   ├── Brand Strategist        — identity systems, category positioning
26: │   └── Visual Designer         — brand assets, high-contrast visual language
27: │
28: ├── DISCIPLINE 3 — EXECUTION SYSTEMS     (Head of Operations)
29: │   ├── Campaign Architect      — funnel architecture, multi-channel launches
30: │   ├── Automation Engineer     — CRM integration, trigger/automation logic
31: │   └── Growth Operator         — deployment, scaling, A/B optimization
32: │
33: └── SUPPORT / CLIENT LAYER (see §5 — gaps to fill)
34:     ├── Client Success Lead     [MISSING — recommended]
35:     ├── Paid Media Buyer        [PARTIAL — fold into Growth Operator]
36:     └── PR & Influence          [MISSING — recommended if expanding]
37: ```
38: 
39: ## 3. Service catalog (every service we can provide)
40: 
41: | # | Service | Discipline | Owner role | Phase | Key deliverable |
42: |---|---------|------------|-----------|-------|-----------------|
43: | 1 | Brand architecture & identity system | Design Architecture | Brand Strategist | Beta | Logo, typography, color psychology map |
44: | 2 | UI/UX & front-end development | Design Architecture | UI/UX Engineer | Beta | Production-ready interfaces, prototypes |
45: | 3 | Market intelligence report | Strategic Intelligence | Market Analyst | Alpha | Market map, blind-spot analysis |
46: | 4 | Competitor vulnerability audit | Strategic Intelligence | Market Analyst | Alpha | SWOT + attack surface |
47: | 5 | Behavioral / persona modeling | Strategic Intelligence | Behavioral Psychologist | Alpha | Persona tiers, messaging psychology |
48: | 6 | Predictive analytics engines | Strategic Intelligence | Data Scientist | Alpha→Gamma | Forecasting models, intent scoring |
49: | 7 | CRM integration & migration | Execution Systems | Automation Engineer | Beta→Gamma | Connected CRM, deterministic routing |
50: | 8 | Marketing automation & triggers | Execution Systems | Automation Engineer | Gamma | Trigger→action workflows, webhooks |
51: | 9 | Conversion funnel architecture | Execution + Design | Campaign Architect | Beta | End-to-end funnel, drop-off fixes |
52: | 10 | Multi-channel campaign deployment | Execution Systems | Campaign Architect | Gamma | Launched campaigns (LinkedIn/Google/Meta/etc.) |
53: | 11 | Paid media & ad operations | Execution Systems | Growth Operator | Gamma | Spend/CTR/CPA management, creative |
54: | 12 | Content & creative production | Design Architecture | Visual Designer | Beta | Ads, landing pages, email, social |
55: | 13 | A/B testing & optimization | Execution Systems | Growth Operator | Gamma | Variants, lift + confidence, winners |
56: | 14 | Strategic positioning & roadmap | Strategic Intelligence | Head of Intelligence | Alpha | Positioning blueprint, 45-day sprint |
57: 
58: ## 4. Methodology ↔ discipline ownership
59: 
60: | Phase | Name | Lead discipline | Output |
61: |-------|------|-----------------|--------|
62: | **Alpha** | Intelligence & Blueprinting | Strategic Intelligence | Market audit, positioning, roadmap |
63: | **Beta** | Structural Development | Design Architecture | Brand system, UI/UX, built assets |
64: | **Gamma** | Deployment & Optimization | Execution Systems | Live campaigns, scaling, optimization |
65: 
66: ## 5. Gaps vs. Ogilvy — recommended additions
67: 
68: | Function | Status | Why it matters |

70: | **Client Success / Account Director** | Missing | Ogilvy retains enterprise clients via dedicated account leads; you currently run accounts founder-direct. Add before scaling beyond ~5 active clients. |
71: | **Paid Media Buyer** | Partial (folded into Growth Operator) | Spend management is a distinct skill; split out once ad spend exceeds ~$20k/mo. |
72: | **PR & Influence** | Missing | Ogilvy's strongest vertical. Optional — only if you expand beyond technical/SMB into category-definition plays. |
73: | **Finance / Ops** | Missing | Invoicing, contracts, vendor accounts. Can stay founder-led early. |
74: 
75: ## 6. Scaling path (grow without becoming Ogilvy)

# BAZventures — Organizational Chart & Service Catalog

> Founder-led, AI-native agency. Triadic collective of senior-only specialists.
> Model contrasted against Ogilvy's matrixed, region × discipline structure.

## 1. Operating model

- **Shape:** Flat, founder-led collective — no regional hubs, no junior layer.
- **Core:** Three fused disciplines (Intelligence, Design, Execution) that mirror the Alpha → Beta → Gamma methodology.
- **Principle:** "No generalists. No junior staff. No learning curves." Every seat is senior and owns a function end-to-end.

## 2. Org chart

```
CEO / Founder — Brahim Amir Zerbout
(AI developer, Linux/security, CRM architect; central operator & final sign-off)

├── DISCIPLINE 1 — STRATEGIC INTELLIGENCE  (Head of Intelligence)
│   ├── Data Scientist          — predictive models, analytics pipelines
│   ├── Market Analyst          — competitive vulnerability audits, market mapping

display.text:
# BAZventures — Organizational Chart & Service Catalog

> Founder-led, AI-native agency. Triadic collective of senior-only specialists.
> Model contrasted against Ogilvy's matrixed, region × discipline structure.

## 1. Operating model

- **Shape:** Flat, founder-led collective — no regional hubs, no junior layer.
- **Core:** Three fused disciplines (Intelligence, Design, Execution) that mirror the Alpha → Beta → Gamma methodology.
- **Principle:** "No generalists. No junior staff. No learning curves." Every seat is senior and owns a function end-to-end.

## 2. Org chart

```
CEO / Founder — Brahim Amir Zerbout
(AI developer, Linux/security, CRM architect; central operator & final sign-off)

├── DISCIPLINE 1 — STRATEGIC INTELLIGENCE  (Head of Intelligence)
│   ├── Data Scientist          — predictive models, analytics pipelines
│   ├── Market Analyst          — competitive vulnerability audits, market mapping
│   └── Behavioral Psychologist — messaging psychology, persona/positioning
│
├── DISCIPLINE 2 — DESIGN ARCHITECTURE   (Head of Design)
│   ├── UI/UX Engineer          — frictionless interfaces, front-end build
│   ├── Brand Strategist        — identity systems, category positioning
│   └── Visual Designer         — brand assets, high-contrast visual language
│
├── DISCIPLINE 3 — EXECUTION SYSTEMS     (Head of Operations)
│   ├── Campaign Architect      — funnel architecture, multi-channel launches
│   ├── Automation Engineer     — CRM integration, trigger/automation logic
│   └── Growth Operator         — deployment, scaling, A/B optimization
│
└── SUPPORT / CLIENT LAYER (see §5 — gaps to fill)
    ├── Client Success Lead     [MISSING — recommended]
    ├── Paid Media Buyer        [PARTIAL — fold into Growth Operator]
    └── PR & Influence          [MISSING — recommended if expanding]
```

## 3. Service catalog (every service we can provide)

| # | Service | Discipline | Owner role | Phase | Key deliverable |
|---|---------|------------|-----------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Brand architecture & identity system | Design Architecture | Brand Strategist | Beta | Logo, typography, color psychology map |
| 2 | UI/UX & front-end development | Design Architecture | UI/UX Engineer | Beta | Production-ready interfaces, prototypes |
| 3 | Market intelligence report | Strategic Intelligence | Market Analyst | Alpha | Market map, blind-spot analysis |
| 4 | Competitor vulnerability audit | Strategic Intelligence | Market Analyst | Alpha | SWOT + attack surface |
| 5 | Behavioral / persona modeling | Strategic Intelligence | Behavioral Psychologist | Alpha | Persona tiers, messaging psychology |
| 6 | Predictive analytics engines | Strategic Intelligence | Data Scientist | Alpha→Gamma | Forecasting models, intent scoring |
| 7 | CRM integration & migration | Execution Systems | Automation Engineer | Beta→Gamma | Connected CRM, deterministic routing |
| 8 | Marketing automation & triggers | Execution Systems | Automation Engineer | Gamma | Trigger→action workflows, webhooks |
| 9 | Conversion funnel architecture | Execution + Design | Campaign Architect | Beta | End-to-end funnel, drop-off fixes |
| 10 | Multi-channel campaign deployment | Execution Systems | Campaign Architect | Gamma | Launched campaigns (LinkedIn/Google/Meta/etc.) |
| 11 | Paid media & ad operations | Execution Systems | Growth Operator | Gamma | Spend/CTR/CPA management, creative |
| 12 | Content & creative production | Design Architecture | Visual Designer | Beta | Ads, landing pages, email, social |
| 13 | A/B testing & optimization | Execution Systems | Growth Operator | Gamma | Variants, lift + confidence, winners |
| 14 | Strategic positioning & roadmap | Strategic Intelligence | Head of Intelligence | Alpha | Positioning blueprint, 45-day sprint |

## 4. Methodology ↔ discipline ownership

| Phase | Name | Lead discipline | Output |
|-------|------|-----------------|--------|
| **Alpha** | Intelligence & Blueprinting | Strategic Intelligence | Market audit, positioning, roadmap |
| **Beta** | Structural Development | Design Architecture | Brand system, UI/UX, built assets |
| **Gamma** | Deployment & Optimization | Execution Systems | Live campaigns, scaling, optimization |

## 5. Gaps vs. Ogilvy — recommended additions

| Function | Status | Why it matters |
|----------|--------|----------------|
| **Client Success / Account Director** | Missing | Ogilvy retains enterprise clients via dedicated account leads; you currently run accounts founder-direct. Add before scaling beyond ~5 active clients. |
| **Paid Media Buyer** | Partial (folded into Growth Operator) | Spend management is a distinct skill; split out once ad spend exceeds ~$20k/mo. |
| **PR & Influence** | Missing | Ogilvy's strongest vertical. Optional — only if you expand beyond technical/SMB into category-definition plays. |
| **Finance / Ops** | Missing | Invoicing, contracts, vendor accounts. Can stay founder-led early. |

## 6. Scaling path (grow without becoming Ogilvy)

1. **Now (founder + collective):** all 14 services, founder as integrator.
2. **Next (3–5 clients):** add **Client Success Lead**; promote one senior per discipline to "Head" (already mapped above).
3. **Later (scale):** spin **Paid Media** into its own seat; optionally add **PR** only if pursuing enterprise/category plays.
4. **Never copy:** Ogilvy's regional matrix — irrelevant below global-enterprise scale. Your edge is speed + technical depth, not headcount.

the web. Each file summarizes key concepts and best practices, then lists the
source URLs so you can go deeper.

> Note: Snippets were gathered via web search. Treat these docs as a starting

## Contents

| File | Topic |
| --- | --- |
| [01-digital-marketing-fundamentals.md](01-digital-marketing-fundamentals.md) | Core channels, media models, funnel, measurement |
| [02-seo.md](02-seo.md) | Search engine optimization (Google-first) |
| [03-email-marketing.md](03-email-marketing.md) | Deliverability, authentication, list health |

display.text:
# Marketing Documentation

A curated set of marketing reference docs compiled from authoritative sources on
the web. Each file summarizes key concepts and best practices, then lists the
source URLs so you can go deeper.

> Note: Snippets were gathered via web search. Treat these docs as a starting
> reference; always verify platform-specific rules against the primary source,
> as they change frequently.

## Contents

| File | Topic |
| --- | --- |
| [01-digital-marketing-fundamentals.md](01-digital-marketing-fundamentals.md) | Core channels, media models, funnel, measurement |
| [02-seo.md](02-seo.md) | Search engine optimization (Google-first) |
| [03-email-marketing.md](03-email-marketing.md) | Deliverability, authentication, list health |
| [04-social-media-marketing.md](04-social-media-marketing.md) | Platform strategy, content, engagement |
| [05-paid-ads-ppc.md](05-paid-ads-ppc.md) | Google Ads structure & campaign best practices |
| [06-content-marketing.md](06-content-marketing.md) | Strategy framework, production, distribution |

## How the channels fit together

The dominant theme across every source: **integration**. No single channel wins
alone. A strong system looks like this:

- **Owned media** (site, blog, email list) — assets you control and that compound.
- **Earned media** (press, shares, reviews, word of mouth) — highest credibility.
- **Paid media** (search/social/display ads) — fast, controllable, stops when budget stops.

SEO feeds content, content fuels social and email, and paid amplifies the best of it.

## The customer funnel (applied digitally)

1. **Awareness** — SEO, social, paid ads.
2. **Consideration** — content, email, reviews.
3. **Conversion** — landing pages, offers, checkout.
4. **Retention & loyalty** — email, community, post-purchase experience.

_Last compiled: 2026-07-10_

> ⚡ **Core Doctrine: Intensity Beats Extensity — Every Time.**
> One channel mastered beats five channels touched. One offer sharpened beats ten offers scattered. One persona deeply understood beats five personas guessed. Read the full principle in [`docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md`](docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md).

A complete marketing platform built with **Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind, and SQLite**. **30 interconnected sections** covering the entire marketing workflow — all running locally, no external API keys required.

## Quick start

```bash
cd ~/marketing-hub
npm install
npm run seed               # populate with 200+ sample records
npm run dev                # http://localhost:3000
```

For production:
```bash
npm run build
npm start

# Marketing Hub — All-in-One Marketing Command Center

> ⚡ **Core Doctrine: Intensity Beats Extensity — Every Time.**
> One channel mastered beats five channels touched. One offer sharpened beats ten offers scattered. One persona deeply understood beats five personas guessed. Read the full principle in [`docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md`](docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md).

A complete marketing platform built with **Next.js 14, TypeScript, Tailwind, and SQLite**. **30 interconnected sections** covering the entire marketing workflow — all running locally, no external API keys required.

## Quick start

```bash
cd ~/marketing-hub
npm install
npm run seed               # populate with 200+ sample records
npm run dev                # http://localhost:3000
```

For production:
```bash
npm run build
npm start
```

## What's inside (30 sections)

### 🧭 Plan
| Section | What it does |
|---|---|
| **Dashboard** | Unified metrics, module grid, traffic/conversion charts, active campaigns, upcoming content |
| **Brand kit** | Logos (primary/dark/icon), color palette, typography, voice & tone, usage guidelines |
| **Personas** | Audience profiles with goals, pain points, channels, messaging. Primary / secondary / anti-persona tiers |
| **Segments** | Rule-based audience segments (e.g. `score > 70 AND tag = newsletter`) |
| **Competitors** | Track competitors: positioning, pricing, SWOT analysis, strengths & weaknesses |
| **Campaigns** | Kanban (draft → scheduled → live → paused → completed), full CRUD, detail page, budget tracking |
| **Funnels** | Visual customer-journey builder — map awareness → landing → form → email → sale. Tracks conversion per step |
| **Budget planner** | Monthly budget by category, planned vs actual, variance tracking, category breakdown chart |
| **Playbooks** | Repeatable SOPs with steps, owners, durations, KPIs. Acquisition / activation / retention / revenue / branding |

### ✏️ Create
| Section | What it does |
|---|---|
| **Content calendar** | Monthly grid view with per-channel color coding, click-to-create |
| **Social studio** | Composer with per-channel character limits (Twitter 280, LinkedIn 3000, etc.), preview, scheduling |
| **Email builder** | Block-based editor (hero, features, CTA, testimonials, pricing, FAQ), live preview, HTML/text export |
| **Landing pages** | Same block editor for marketing pages, live preview, SEO meta, static HTML export |
| **Forms** | Drag-style form builder — text/email/phone/textarea/select/checkbox/number/URL fields, submit tracking |
| **Lead magnets** | Library of eBooks, templates, checklists, webinars, coupons, videos — track downloads |
| **Copy generator** | Offline template-based: headlines, hooks, CTAs, ad copy, social posts, email sequences, blog outlines |
| **Asset library** | File upload, image/video/document previews, search, tags |

### 📈 Grow
| Section | What it does |
|---|---|
| **SEO toolkit** | Keyword tracker (volume/difficulty/opportunity), HTML auditor, sitemap.xml, robots.txt |
| **Ads manager** | Multi-channel (Meta/Google/TikTok/LinkedIn), spend/impressions/clicks/CTR/CPA, pause/resume |
| **Events & webinars** | Webinars, conferences, meetups, workshops, launches — registration tracking, capacity fill rate |
| **CRM & deals** | Contacts with scoring, companies, deals kanban (drag between stages), activity tracking |
| **Automations** | Trigger→action rule builder (8 triggers × 8 actions), run tracking, pause/resume |
| **A/B testing** | Experiments with two variants, traffic & conversions, auto-computed lift & confidence, winner flag |
| **Testimonials** | Social proof library with ratings, sources (G2/Trustpilot/Twitter/email/manual), featured toggle, avatars |
| **Conversations inbox** | Unified inbox — DMs, comments, replies, emails. Channel filters, sentiment badges, star, search, reply |
| **Surveys (NPS, CSAT)** | NPS (0-10), CSAT (1-5), CES (1-7), custom — track responses & aggregate score |
| **Analytics** | Multi-range charts (traffic/conversions/revenue), funnel viz, channel mix, per-channel performance |

### ⚙️ Manage
| Section | What it does |
|---|---|
| **Tasks** | To-do tracker with priority, due date, project tagging |
| **Integrations** | 24 service connectors (Mailchimp, SendGrid, HubSpot, Meta, Google, Slack, etc.) with connect/disconnect flow |
| **Reports** | Auto-generated performance summaries, CSV export, jsPDF PDF export |
| **Settings** | Workspace, brand color, email defaults, API key vault (encrypted at rest) |

## Architecture

- **Next.js 14 App Router** with server components + client islands
- **SQLite** (`better-sqlite3`) for everything — single-file database at `data/hub.sqlite`
- **Tailwind CSS** + custom design system (no UI library), dynamic color safelist
- **Recharts** for all charts
- **lucide-react** for icons
- **framer-motion** for premium animations
- **jsPDF** for PDF reports
- **TypeScript** strict mode throughout
- RESTful API routes under `/api/*`
- Full CRUD on every resource

## Database schema (35 tables)

`users, workspaces, settings, contacts, companies, deals, activities, campaigns, content, emails, landing_pages, seo_keywords, seo_audits, ads, assets, automations, integrations, api_keys, copy_templates, notes, tasks, brand_assets, personas, segments, forms, form_submissions, lead_magnets, funnels, experiments, testimonials, events, event_registrations, competitors, conversations, budget_items, playbooks, surveys, survey_responses`

## File map

```
src/
├── app/                              # 35 page routes
│   ├── page.tsx                      # Dashboard with module grid
│   ├── brand/                        # Brand kit (logos, colors, type, voice)
│   ├── personas/                     # Audience personas
│   ├── segments/                     # Rule-based segments
│   ├── forms/                        # Form builder
│   ├── lead-magnets/                 # eBooks, templates, etc.
│   ├── funnels/                      # Visual customer journeys

│   ├── events/                       # Webinars & conferences
│   ├── competitors/                  # Competitive intelligence + SWOT
│   ├── inbox/                        # Unified conversations
│   ├── budget/                       # Spend planner
│   ├── playbooks/                    # SOPs
│   ├── surveys/                      # NPS, CSAT, CES
│   ├── campaigns/[id]/page.tsx       # Campaign detail
│   ├── emails/[id]/page.tsx          # Email builder
│   ├── landing-pages/[id]/page.tsx   # Landing page builder
│   ├── preview/[id]/page.tsx         # Iframe preview
│   └── api/                          # 71+ API routes (full CRUD on every resource)
├── components/
│   ├── Sidebar.tsx                   # 30 modules in 4 groups
│   ├── Topbar.tsx, Modal.tsx, Stat.tsx, Badge.tsx
│   ├── BlockEditor.tsx               # Block-based content editor
│   ├── CommandPalette.tsx            # ⌘K with all 30 sections
│   ├── useFetch.ts, Anim.tsx, Skeleton.tsx, Toast.tsx, ThemeProvider.tsx
└── lib/
    ├── db/index.ts                   # SQLite + 35-table schema + auto-migrations
    ├── api-helpers.ts                # listRows, insertRow, updateRow, deleteRow
    ├── constants.ts                  # Channels, deal stages, integrations, formatters
    ├── blocks.ts                     # Block model + HTML renderers
    ├── copy.ts                       # Copy generation (headlines, hooks, CTAs, etc.)
    ├── seo.ts                        # SEO audit, sitemap, robots
    └── analytics.ts                  # Deterministic mock data for charts

scripts/seed.ts                       # Seeds 250+ records across all 30 sections
data/hub.sqlite                       # SQLite database (auto-created)
```

## Verified working

All 35 pages and 71+ APIs return 200. Tested:
- Dashboard, brand kit, personas, segments, forms, lead magnets, funnels, experiments, testimonials, events, competitors, inbox, budget, playbooks, surveys
- All existing: campaigns, calendar, studio, emails, landing pages, copy, SEO, ads, CRM, analytics, automations, assets, integrations, reports, settings, tasks
- Detail pages (campaigns/[id], emails/[id], landing-pages/[id], preview/[id])
- Create / Read / Update / Delete on every new resource
- Form submissions increment leads_count
- Survey responses auto-recompute aggregate score
- A/B test lift + confidence auto-computed
- ⌘K palette shows all 30 sections + searches across all records
- Module grid on dashboard with live counts from each section

## Coverage matrix

| Marketing area | Hub coverage |
|---|---|
| Strategy & planning | Brand kit, Personas, Competitors, Playbooks, Funnels |
| Audience & segmentation | Personas, Segments, CRM |
| Content creation | Calendar, Studio, Email builder, Landing pages, Forms, Copy generator |
| Distribution & channels | Social, Email, Landing pages, Forms, Events, Inbox |
| Lead capture | Forms, Lead magnets, Landing pages |
| Conversion & sales | Funnels, Experiments, A/B tests |
| Paid acquisition | Ads manager, Budget |
| Earned & owned | SEO, Content, Email, Events |
| Customer feedback | Surveys, Testimonials, Inbox |
| Operations & measurement | Analytics, Reports, Budget, Tasks, Integrations |

→ **30 sections, 35 DB tables, 71 API routes, 250+ seeded records.**

<content>
1: # Intensity Beats Extensity — Every Time
2: 
3: > **The core marketing doctrine for all projects.**
4: 
5: ---
6: 
7: ## The Principle
8: 
9: **One channel, mastered deeply, will outperform five channels covered shallowly. Every. Single. Time.**
10: 
11: This isn't a preference. It's a law of focus. Marketing success comes from:
12: 
13: 1. **Depth of message** — One offer, refined to razor sharpness, hitting the exact nerve of the exact persona
14: 2. **Depth of channel** — One platform understood at the algorithm level, not the surface level
15: 3. **Depth of frequency** — Showing up consistently in one place until you're unavoidable, not flickering across many
16: 4. **Depth of creative** — One piece of content so good it travels on its own, not ten pieces that need pushing
17: 
18: ## What This Means in Practice
19: 
20: ### ❌ Extensity (what we don't do)
21: - Launching on 7 social platforms simultaneously
22: - Running Google Ads + Meta Ads + TikTok + LinkedIn Ads all at once with thin budgets
23: - Writing blog posts for SEO, social posts for engagement, email sequences for nurture, and a podcast for authority — all half-baked
24: - Spreading $5K/month across 10 channels ($500 each, none getting traction)
25: - Copy-pasting the same content across 6 platforms with minor tweaks
26: 
27: ### ✅ Intensity (what we do)
28: - **Pick ONE channel** that matches where the persona actually lives
29: - **Pour the full budget** into that one channel until it compounds
30: - **Create ONE hero piece** of content per week so good it would be embarrassing to competitor
31: - **Run ONE ad campaign** with enough daily spend to get statistical significance in days, not months
32: - **Build ONE funnel** and optimize every step until conversion is best-in-class
33: - **Dominate one keyword cluster** before touching a second
34: 
35: ## The Intensity Checklist
36: 
37: Before approving any marketing project, ask:
38: 
39: - [ ] **Is this focused on ONE primary channel?** If more than two, cut.
40: - [ ] **Is the budget concentrated enough to generate real signal?** Thin spreads produce noise, not data.
41: - [ ] **Is there ONE hero offer being pushed?** Multiple offers dilute attention and confuse tracking.
42: - [ ] **Is the creative quality high enough to win on its own merit?** If it needs paid distribution to be seen, it's not intense enough.
43: - [ ] **Are we going deep on one persona before expanding?** Message-market fit with one audience before adding a second.
44: - [ ] **Is there a single primary KPI we're optimizing for?** Not 12 vanity metrics. One number that matters.
45: 
46: ## How This Applies to the Marketing Hub
47:

55: | **Phase 4: Expand** | ONLY after channel #1 is compounding, add channel #2 | Repeat Phase 1-3 for new channel |

# Intensity Beats Extensity — Every Time

> **The core marketing doctrine for all projects.**

---

## The Principle

**One channel, mastered deeply, will outperform five channels covered shallowly. Every. Single. Time.**

This isn't a preference. It's a law of focus. Marketing success comes from:

1. **Depth of message** — One offer, refined to razor sharpness, hitting the exact nerve of the exact persona
2. **Depth of channel** — One platform understood at the algorithm level, not the surface level
3. **Depth of frequency** — Showing up consistently in one place until you're unavoidable, not flickering across many
4. **Depth of creative** — One piece of content so good it travels on its own, not ten pieces that need pushing

## What This Means in Practice

### ❌ Extensity (what we don't do)

display.text:
# Intensity Beats Extensity — Every Time

> **The core marketing doctrine for all projects.**

---

## The Principle

**One channel, mastered deeply, will outperform five channels covered shallowly. Every. Single. Time.**

This isn't a preference. It's a law of focus. Marketing success comes from:

1. **Depth of message** — One offer, refined to razor sharpness, hitting the exact nerve of the exact persona
2. **Depth of channel** — One platform understood at the algorithm level, not the surface level
3. **Depth of frequency** — Showing up consistently in one place until you're unavoidable, not flickering across many
4. **Depth of creative** — One piece of content so good it travels on its own, not ten pieces that need pushing

## What This Means in Practice

### ❌ Extensity (what we don't do)
- Launching on 7 social platforms simultaneously
- Running Google Ads + Meta Ads + TikTok + LinkedIn Ads all at once with thin budgets
- Writing blog posts for SEO, social posts for engagement, email sequences for nurture, and a podcast for authority — all half-baked
- Spreading $5K/month across 10 channels ($500 each, none getting traction)
- Copy-pasting the same content across 6 platforms with minor tweaks

### ✅ Intensity (what we do)
- **Pick ONE channel** that matches where the persona actually lives
- **Pour the full budget** into that one channel until it compounds
- **Create ONE hero piece** of content per week so good it would be embarrassing to competitor
- **Run ONE ad campaign** with enough daily spend to get statistical significance in days, not months
- **Build ONE funnel** and optimize every step until conversion is best-in-class
- **Dominate one keyword cluster** before touching a second

## The Intensity Checklist

Before approving any marketing project, ask:

- [ ] **Is this focused on ONE primary channel?** If more than two, cut.
- [ ] **Is the budget concentrated enough to generate real signal?** Thin spreads produce noise, not data.
- [ ] **Is there ONE hero offer being pushed?** Multiple offers dilute attention and confuse tracking.
- [ ] **Is the creative quality high enough to win on its own merit?** If it needs paid distribution to be seen, it's not intense enough.
- [ ] **Are we going deep on one persona before expanding?** Message-market fit with one audience before adding a second.
- [ ] **Is there a single primary KPI we're optimizing for?** Not 12 vanity metrics. One number that matters.

## How This Applies to the Marketing Hub

The Marketing Hub has 30 sections. That's the toolkit. But **no single campaign should activate all 30 simultaneously.**

| Phase | Intensity Focus | What We Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1: Dominate** | Pick the highest-ROI channel + persona combo | Personas, Campaigns, Copy Generator, one channel in Social Studio |
| **Phase 2: Convert** | Build the world's best funnel for that channel | Landing Pages, Forms, A/B Testing, CRM, Automations |
| **Phase 3: Measure** | Know that one channel better than anyone | Analytics, Attribution, SEO Toolkit (for organic) or Ads Manager (for paid) |
| **Phase 4: Expand** | ONLY after channel #1 is compounding, add channel #2 | Repeat Phase 1-3 for new channel |

## The Anti-Pattern We Reject

> "We need to be everywhere our customers might be."

No. You need to be **unmissable** in the one place they definitely are. Being everywhere means being nowhere. Being nowhere means being forgotten. Being forgotten means being irrelevant.

## The Rule of One

- **One** persona to start
- **One** channel to dominate
- **One** offer to push
- **One** hero creative per cycle
- **One** KPI to optimize
- **One** funnel to perfect

Master the one. Then — and only then — add the second.

---

*This principle governs all marketing projects, campaigns, and strategies. When in doubt, go deeper, not wider.*

metadata.preview:
# Digital Marketing Fundamentals

## Definition

Digital marketing is the promotion of products, services, or brands through
internet-connected channels and technologies — search engines, social media,
email, websites, and mobile apps. The American Marketing Association defines it
broadly as "any marketing methods conducted through electronic devices which
utilize some form of a computer."

The defining difference from traditional marketing is **measurability**: nearly
every click, open, and purchase is trackable, turning marketing from guesswork
into a discipline.

## The durable fundamentals (they outlast tactics)

1. **Core channels** — SEO, content, social, email, paid ads, affiliate/influencer.
2. **The owned / earned / paid media model.**
3. **A data & measurement loop.**
4. **The customer funnel applied across digital touchpoints.**

display.text:
# Digital Marketing Fundamentals

## Definition

Digital marketing is the promotion of products, services, or brands through
internet-connected channels and technologies — search engines, social media,
email, websites, and mobile apps. The American Marketing Association defines it
broadly as "any marketing methods conducted through electronic devices which
utilize some form of a computer."

The defining difference from traditional marketing is **measurability**: nearly
every click, open, and purchase is trackable, turning marketing from guesswork
into a discipline.

## The durable fundamentals (they outlast tactics)

1. **Core channels** — SEO, content, social, email, paid ads, affiliate/influencer.
2. **The owned / earned / paid media model.**
3. **A data & measurement loop.**
4. **The customer funnel applied across digital touchpoints.**

Tools and platforms change; these foundations don't.

## The core channels

| Channel | What it does | Character |
| --- | --- | --- |
| SEO | Earn organic visibility when people search | Durable, high-trust, compounds (3–6+ mo) |
| Content marketing | Attract by being useful, not interruptive | Compounds; fuels other channels |
| Social media | Build audience, community, brand | Discovery + relationship |
| Email | Nurture an owned audience directly | Highest ROI (~$36–42 per $1) |
| Paid ads / PPC | Buy attention & traffic | Fast, controllable; stops with budget |
| Affiliate / influencer | Reach via partners' borrowed trust | Leverage others' audiences |

## Owned, Earned, Paid media

- **Owned** — your website, blog, email list, app. You control them; they compound.
- **Earned** — press, shares, reviews, word of mouth. Can't buy directly; most credible.
- **Paid** — ads across search/social/display. Fast and controllable but rented.

A strong strategy balances all three: paid rents demand, owned builds assets,
earned grants credibility.

## The data & measurement loop

- Visibility into what works lets you find and fix drop-off points.
- Modern principle: **"measure what matters, respect the customer, act on the
  data you can ethically collect"** — not "track everything" (privacy changes,
  cookie restrictions, regulation).
- Metrics that matter: CAC (customer acquisition cost), ROAS (return on ad
  spend), CPL (cost per lead), CLV/LTV (lifetime value), conversion rate.
- ROI formula: `(Revenue - Marketing Cost) / Marketing Cost × 100`.
- Rule of thumb benchmarks cited: aim for ~3:1 ROAS on paid, ~5:1 on organic.

## A starter framework

| Step | Focus | Why |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Fundamentals | Understand channels, media model, data, funnel |
| 2 | One channel | Build real competence in a single discipline |
| 3 | A real project | Apply with actual stakes and feedback |
| 4 | Analytics | Set up measurement to learn what works |
| 5 | Integration | Add channels and connect them into one system |

## 8-step strategy framework

1. Define business objectives & SMART marketing goals.
2. Understand your audience deeply.
3. Audit your current digital presence.
4. Select your channel mix.
5. Develop your content strategy.
6. Set budget and allocate resources.
7. Implement tracking & attribution.
8. Launch, measure, and optimize.

## What's changing in 2026 (AI era)

- Discovery is going **visual and conversational**: Google AI Overviews (~16% of
  queries), ChatGPT search, Perplexity, Gemini, and platform-native search
  (TikTok, YouTube, Reddit).
- New optimization layers: **AEO** (Answer Engine Optimization — get cited by AI
  engines) and **GEO** (Generative Engine Optimization — content shaped for AI
  synthesis).
- Privacy-first marketing and first-party data ownership are now essential.
- The fundamentals stay the same; execution becomes faster, more personalized,
  more visual.

## Typical result timelines

- Paid (PPC/social ads): leads within 24–48 hours.
- Social organic: engagement in days, conversion tuning 2–4 weeks.
- SEO: meaningful movement in 3–6 months, big gains 6–12 months.
- Email (existing list): immediate.

## Sources

- What is digital marketing? — Adobe: https://business.adobe.com/blog/basics/digital-marketing
- Digital Marketing Complete Guide (2026) — PxlPeak: https://pxlpeak.com/guide/digital-marketing-complete-guide
- Fundamentals of Digital Marketing (2026) — BrandClickX: https://brandclickx.com/fundamentals-of-digital-marketing/
- Digital Marketing: The Complete 2026 Guide — Frostbite: https://frostbitemarketing.com/resources/guides/digital-marketing-guide-2026/
- What Is Digital Marketing? A Complete 2026 Guide — Three Girls Media: https://threegirlsmedia.co.uk/digital-marketing-explained-a-complete-2026-guide/
- Ultimate Guide to Digital Marketing (PDF) — DigitalMarketer: https://www.digitalmarketer.com/digital-marketing/assets/pdf/ultimate-guide-to-digital-marketing.pdf

Founder-led AI-native agency with a 30-section all-in-one Marketing Hub product. Plus a service catalog of 14 services. Plus many side projects (empire, nova-with-bank, FlowDeck, brand-aether, bazdev, etc.).

Seth Godin would immediately point at:
1. "The growth partner for ambitious businesses" is a nobody-slogan. Average. Safe. Not a Purple Cow. It's a me-too. Who is the smallest viable audience? "ambitious businesses" is everyone and therefore no one.
2. You're selling a tool (Marketing Hub, 30 sections) AND an agency. That's two different businesses with two different promises. Spread thin.
3. The org chart lists 14 services — "we do everything." Godin: average. Safe. The purple cow is specialization, not breadth.
4. Many side projects (empire, nova, FlowDeck, aether) — the Dip says: most of these will fail because you won't push through the dip on any. Scattered.
5. Intensity beats extensity doc is already inside their own product — but they violate it across the portfolio.
6. No tribe. No permission asset (email list). Who are you leading?
7. Brand is built but not shipped to a market. Lots of assets, no audience.
8. The product "does 30 things" — that's a Swiss army knife, not a purple cow. It's for the maker, not the customer's job-to-be-done.

data.text:
I inspected the actual artifacts in your workspace — `marketing-docs/bazventures-org-chart.md`, `marketing-hub/README.md`, `docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md`, `BAZ-Brand-Assets/BRAND-STANDARD.md`, and your brand config (`tagline: "The growth partner for ambitious businesses."`).

Here's the inspection, in Seth's voice.

## The diagnosis

**You built a Swiss Army knife and called it a strategy.** Thirty sections, fourteen services, a brand system, an org chart, a command center. That's not a Purple Cow. That's a catalog. A purple cow is the *one* thing so remarkable people can't ignore it. You have zero of those and thirty of "fine."

**"The growth partner for ambitious businesses" is a nobody-sentence.** It describes every agency on Earth. Safe. Average. Forgettable. The market doesn't reward "for everyone" — it rewards "for *them*." Who is your smallest viable audience? You haven't named one. "Ambitious businesses" is a census, not a customer.

**You are running two businesses and calling it one.** You're an AI-native agency *and* you're shipping a 30-module SaaS (Marketing Hub) *and* you've got `empire`, `nova-with-bank`, `FlowDeck`, `brand-aether`, `bazdev` sitting in the same directory. That's extensity — the exact disease your own `INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md` warns against. You wrote the cure and didn't take it.

**Your best idea is buried in a docs folder.** "Intensity beats extensity" is a genuinely Seth-Godin principle and it's *internal*. It should be the billboard on the front of the building, not a markdown file inside the tool.

**You have assets, not a tribe.** Brand guidelines, logos, color psychology maps, personas — all made. None of it is in front of a human who chose to hear from you. You have zero permission asset (no email list, no community) and zero public narrative. You optimized the factory and skipped the market.

**Your org chart is a wish, not a plan.** "Client Success — MISSING. PR — MISSING. Finance — MISSING." You can't serve five clients the way you describe serving one. Most of those 14 services will be delivered founder-direct, at 2am, by you. That's not a business. That's a burnout schedule with a logo.

## Exactly what needs to be done

1. **Name the smallest viable audience — in one sentence.** Not "ambitious businesses." Pick the narrowest group you can win: e.g. "AI-native founders doing $20k–$100k/mo who hate agencies." Write it on the wall. Every decision after this serves only them.

2. **Kill 80% of the menu.** Choose *one* hero offer (one service, one outcome, one price) and delete the other 13 from the homepage. Breadth is the tell of someone who's afraid to pick. Pick.

3. **Decide: agency or product. Not both this quarter.** Marketing Hub is a separate company with a separate promise. Either it's the business or it's a tool you use. Right now it's the thing eating the time that should go to clients. Choose one to ship first.

4. **Bury the side projects.** `empire`, `nova-with-bank`, `FlowDeck`, `brand-aether` — most will die in the Dip anyway. Park all but one. The Dip is real; you can't push through five at once. Pick the one with a real customer and ignore the rest for 90 days.

5. **Turn your own principle into the brand.** Make "Intensity beats extensity" the public face — the tagline, the manifesto, the first thing a prospect reads. It's your only remarkable idea. Use it.

6. **Build one permission asset before one more logo.** Start the email list / community for that smallest audience. Ship a weekly note that's so good they forward it. Earned attention compounds; a 30-section dashboard does not.

7. **Write the story, not the spec.** Replace the feature-grid homepage with a narrative: who it's for, the enemy (scattered marketing), and the transformation. People don't buy 35 database tables. They buy a better version of themselves.

8. **Prove it with one channel, relentlessly.** Per your own rule: one persona, one channel, one offer, one KPI. Dominate that before touching a second. Show up until you're unavoidable.

9. **Add the missing human seat now, not at 5 clients.** A client-success/account lead isn't optional growth — it's what lets you sleep and keeps clients. Founder-direct doesn't scale past you.

The one-line version: **you're remarkable at the *idea* of focus and average at applying it. Pick the one, ship it to the few, lead them, and ignore the rest.**

+// BAZ Refocus — single source of truth for the relaunch.
+// One audience. One offer. One channel. (See docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md)
+//
+// This file IS the strategy. Every page below imports it. Change the business

+
+export const positioning = {
+  // PART 1 — The smallest viable audience.
+  // Not "ambitious businesses". A census is not a customer.
+  audience: {
+    name: "Technical founders & solo operators",
+    who: "AI-native / software founders doing $20k–$250k/mo who build the product themselves",
+    pains: [
+      "Hate slow, junior-staffed agencies that send decks instead of results",
+      "Don't have time to become a marketer and don't trust generalists",
+      "Tried 5 channels at once, got noise, not signal",
+    ],
+    want: "One senior team that ships their entire marketing system and gets out of the way",
+    antiAudience: "Enterprise procurement, brand-only plays, anyone who needs 3 quotes and a committee",
+  },
+
+  // PART 2 — Kill 80% of the menu. One hero offer.
+  // The other 13 services still exist; they are NOT on the homepage.
+  heroOffer: {
+    id: "system-in-45",
+    name: "Your marketing system, built and running in 45 days",
+    promise: "One senior team. One channel. One funnel. Live in 45 days — or you don't pay.",
+    price: "Flat $9,500 + 10% of attributed lift, first 90 days",
+    outcome: "A compounding owned channel (email/SEO) + one paid channel at statistical significance",
+    // The 13 services we deliberately HIDE from the front door:
+    hiddenServices: [
+      "Brand architecture & identity system",
+      "UI/UX & front-end development",
+      "Market intelligence report",
+      "Competitor vulnerability audit",
+      "Behavioral / persona modeling",
+      "Predictive analytics engines",
+      "CRM integration & migration",
+      "Marketing automation & triggers",
+      "Conversion funnel architecture",
+      "Multi-channel campaign deployment",
+      "Paid media & ad operations",
+      "Content & creative production",
+      "A/B testing & optimization",
+      "Strategic positioning & roadmap",
+    ],
+  },
+
+  // PART 3 — Agency or product? This quarter, the agency leads; the Hub is the tool.
+  decision: {
+    lead: "agency",
+    note: "Marketing Hub is the weapon BAZ uses to deliver. It is NOT the product we sell this quarter. Selling both at once is extensity.",
+    productStatus: "internal tool — used on every client, not marketed",
+  },
+
+  // PART 5 — The brand, rebuilt around our one remarkable idea.
+  brand: {
+    name: "BAZ",
+    oldTagline: "The growth partner for ambitious businesses.",
+    tagline: "Intensity beats extensity. We market like it.",
+    manifesto: "One audience. One channel. One offer. Go deep, not wide.",
+    principle: "Intensity beats extensity — every time.",
+  },
+
+  // PART 8 — Prove it on ONE channel first.
+  channel: {
+    primary: "Founder-led email + LinkedIn (owned + earned)",
+    kpi: "Replies & calls booked from the list — not impressions",
+    expandOnlyAfter: "Primary channel compounds for 90 days",
+  },
+};
+
+export type Positioning = typeof positioning;

input.content:
# Part 1 — The Smallest Viable Audience

> Seth's rule: pick the smallest group you can win, then dominate it. "Ambitious
> businesses" is everyone, which means it's no one. Here is the narrowest real
> market BAZ can own.

## Audience (one sentence)
**AI-native and software founders doing $20k–$250k/mo who build the product
themselves and refuse to babysit a junior-staffed agency.**

## Why this group
- They are technical (matches the founder's actual edge: AI dev, Linux/security, CRM).
- They already distrust generalist agencies — we don't have to convince them, only prove it.
- They ship fast and respect speed; our model ("no juniors, no learning curves") is the product.
- They have budget but not time — a flat, done-for-them offer converts.

## What they hate (our enemy in the story)
1. Agencies that send decks instead of results.
2. Generalists who guess at their market.
3. Spreading thin across 5 channels and getting noise.

## What they want
One senior team that builds the whole marketing system and gets out of the way.

## Who is NOT the audience (say no loudly)
- Enterprise procurement with committees and 3-quote processes.
- Pure brand/logo plays with no performance pressure.
- Anyone who needs to be educated on why focus wins.

## The one hero offer (Part 2)
**"Your marketing system, built and running in 45 days."**
Flat $9,500 + 10% of attributed lift for 90 days. One channel. One funnel.
Live in 45 days or you don't pay.

The other 13 services still exist and get used — but they are never the
front door. Breadth is the tell of someone afraid to pick.

input.content:
# Part 3 — Agency or Product? The Decision.

You are running two businesses under one roof and calling it one. That is
extensity — the exact disease `docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md` warns against.

## The two businesses
1. **BAZ the agency** — founder-led, AI-native, senior-only. Sells outcomes.
2. **Marketing Hub the product** — a 30-section all-in-one command center. Sells software.

Different promises. Different buyers. Different sales motions. Doing both at
once splits the founder's attention and dilutes the story.

## The decision (this quarter)
- **Lead: the agency.** It has a real, payable offer and a real edge.
- **Marketing Hub: internal tool.** It is the weapon BAZ uses to deliver the
  45-day system faster than any competitor. It is NOT marketed this quarter.
- **Trigger to revisit:** only when the agency has a waitlist AND a client-success
  lead (Part 9) running renewals — then spin the Hub into its own company.

## What this means in code
`assets/positioning.ts → decision` encodes it:
```ts
decision: {
  lead: "agency",
  productStatus: "internal tool — used on every client, not marketed",
}
```
The landing page (`index.html`) sells the agency offer. The Hub is never linked
from the front door this quarter.

+  lead (Part 9) running renewals — then spin the Hub into its own company.
+
+## What this means in code
+`assets/positioning.ts → decision` encodes it:
+```ts
+decision: {
+  lead: "agency",
+  productStatus: "internal tool — used on every client, not marketed",
+}
+```
+The landing page (`index.html`) sells the agency offer. The Hub is never linked
+from the front door this quarter.

# Part 4 — Park the Side Projects

The Dip is real: most projects die because no one pushes through the hard middle.
You cannot push through five at once. Park all but the one with a real customer.

## Rule
One active bet. Everything else is explicitly **paused** and dated, so it's a
decision, not drift.

## Status board
| Project | Status | Reason | Revisit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BAZ agency / Marketing Hub delivery** | ACTIVE | Real clients, real revenue | — |
| `empire` | PARKED | No customer, no offer | 2026-Q4 |
| `nova-with-bank` | PARKED | Side experiment, no market proof | 2026-Q4 |
| `FlowDeck` | PARKED | Tool, not a customer | 2027-Q1 |
| `brand-aether` | PARKED | Brand exploration, not a business | 2027-Q1 |
| `bazdev` | PARKED | Internal dev scratch | 2027-Q1 |
| `baz-agent-system` | PARKED | Interesting, no buyer yet | 2026-Q4 |
| `baz-corpus` | PARKED | Asset, not a product | 2027-Q1 |

## How to un-park
A parked project may be promoted to ACTIVE only when ALL are true:
1. The agency has a waitlist (proves the founder's time is the constraint).
2. A client-success lead owns renewals (Part 9).
3. The project has one named customer who paid or committed.

Otherwise: leave it parked. Intensity beats extensity.

input.content:
# Part 8 — The One-Channel Playbook

Prove the model on ONE channel before touching a second. Per `INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md`,
spread budgets produce noise, not data.

## The Rule of One (this 90-day cycle)
- **One audience** — technical founders (Part 1).
- **one channel** — founder-led email + LinkedIn (owned + earned).
- **one offer** — "Your marketing system in 45 days" (Part 2).
- **one hero creative per week** — one essay so good it travels without paid push.
- **one KPI** — replies & calls booked from the list. Not impressions. Not followers.

## The sequence
1. **Week 1** — Stand up the permission asset (Part 6). Zero subscribers → goal: 100.
2. **Week 2–4** — One remarkable essay/week. Publish to email + LinkedIn. No other channel.
3. **Week 5–8** — Double down on the format/angle that got the most replies. Kill the rest.
4. **Week 9–12** — One paid test (LinkedIn) ONLY on the proven message, enough daily
   spend to reach significance in days. Expand ONLY after the channel compounds.

## What we do NOT do (extensity)
- No TikTok, no Meta, no Google Ads, no podcast — until the primary channel compounds.
- No blog-for-SEO spam, no 6-platform cross-post, no "be everywhere" thinking.
- No second offer. The 13 hidden services stay hidden.

## The number that matters
`calls_booked_from_list / subscribers`. If that moves, the business works. If it
doesn't, we change the message — not the channel count.

input.content:
# Part 9 — The Missing Seat: Client Success Lead

The org chart says it plainly: **Client Success / Account Director — MISSING.**
Founder-direct account management does not scale past you, and it is why you
can't sleep. This is not optional growth; it's the thing that lets the business
exist without you in the room.

## The role (hire or promote before client #5)
- **Owns:** the client relationship post-sale, onboarding, monthly results review,
  renewal, and upsell of the 13 hidden services (once a client trusts the first win).
- **Does NOT own:** delivery. Senior specialists deliver. This person protects the
  client from founder burnout and protects the founder from being the bottleneck.
- **Single KPI:** net revenue retention. A renewed client is the only proof.

## Why now, not later
The `bazventures-org-chart.md` says "add before scaling beyond ~5 active clients."
You are at the edge of that. Adding this seat turns 14 services into a renewal
engine instead of a founder to-do list.

## Spec (for the posting)
- 5+ yrs client/account leadership at a senior-only or boutique shop.
- Comfortable saying no to scopes that break the one-offer rule (Part 2).
- Writes the monthly results note clients actually read.
- Reports to founder; owns the renewal number.

## In code
This role is reflected in `assets/positioning.ts` is intentionally absent — the
positioning file describes the *market* face (one audience, one offer). The seat
is an *internal* hire. Track it in the org chart, not the homepage.

input.content:
BAZ Offer Manifesto Focus Playbook Join
Intensity
beats extensity.

Every time. One audience, deeply known. One channel, mastered. One offer, sharpened until it cuts. Go deep, not wide — or don't bother.

This is not a preference. It's a law of focus.

Marketing fails when it's spread. Five channels touched beat none mastered. We chose the one.

Extensity (what we reject)

Seven platforms at once. Ten services on the homepage. A budget sliced so thin it buys noise. Being everywhere means being nowhere.

Intensity (what we do)

One persona, understood to the bone. One channel, owned at the algorithm level. One hero piece a week so good it travels without being pushed.

The rule of one

One audience to start. One channel to dominate. One offer to push. One hero creative per cycle. One KPI to optimize. One funnel to perfect. Master the one — then, and only then, add the second.

This principle lives in docs/INTENSITY_PRINCIPLE.md. We stopped keeping it internal.

BAZ — the 45-day marketing system for technical founders.

BAZ Manifesto Focus Playbook Join the list
For technical founders, not committees

Your marketing system — built and running in 45 days.

One senior team. One channel. One funnel. No juniors, no learning curves, no deck that ends in "let's circle back." Live in 45 days or you don't pay.

Get the 45-day plan Read the manifesto

You didn't start a company to become a marketer.

You built the product. You shipped it. Now you're told to "be everywhere" — so you spread across five channels, hire a generalist agency that sends decks, and watch the noise roll in.

That's not marketing. That's extensity. And it loses every time.

We do the opposite.

We pick the one audience you actually serve, the one channel they live in, and the one offer that moves them. Then we go deep until you're unmissable there — and nowhere else. That's the whole philosophy.

One offer. That's it.

We do fourteen things. You don't need to hear about thirteen of them. This is the door.

  • One owned channel (email/SEO) that compounds, plus one paid channel at statistical significance.
  • One funnel, optimized step-by-step until conversion is best-in-class.
  • Live in 45 days — or you don't pay.

Join the list. One email a week, no spam.

We publish one remarkable essay a week for technical founders who'd rather ship than sit in meetings. That's the whole channel. No funnel-bait.

No tracking pixels. No reselling. Unsubscribe in one click.

BAZ — Intensity beats extensity. Manifesto · Playbook · What we parked

input.content:
BAZ Offer Manifesto Focus Playbook Join
The rule of one

Prove it on one channel. Then add the second.

Spread budgets buy noise, not data. This 90‑day cycle has exactly one audience, one channel, one offer, one KPI.

One audience

Technical founders (Part 1).

One channel

Founder-led email + LinkedIn (owned + earned).

one offer

"Your marketing system in 45 days."

one KPI

Calls booked from the list — not impressions.

The sequence

  • Week 1 — Stand up the permission asset. Goal: 100 subscribers.
  • Week 2–4 — One remarkable essay/week to email + LinkedIn. Nothing else.
  • Week 5–8 — Double down on the format that got the most replies. Kill the rest.
  • Week 9–12 — One paid test on the proven message, enough spend for significance in days.

What we do NOT do

  • No TikTok / Meta / Google Ads / podcast until the primary channel compounds.
  • No blog-spam, no 6-platform cross-post, no "be everywhere."
  • No second offer. The 13 hidden services stay hidden.

The number that matters

calls_booked_from_list / subscribers. If it moves, the business works. If not, we change the message — not the channel count.

Full plan in strategy/one-channel-playbook.md.

BAZ — back to the offer

input.content:
# BAZ Refocus — code for all 9 parts

Built from the Seth Godin inspection. One audience, one offer, one channel.
Zero dependencies (Node built-ins only).

## Run it
```bash
cd ~/baz-refocus
node server.js          # → http://localhost:4173
```
Test the permission asset:
```bash
curl -X POST localhost:4173/api/subscribe \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"name":"Ada","email":"ada@founder.dev","source":"test"}'
curl localhost:4173/api/subscribers
```

## The 9 parts → files
| # | Part | File |
|---|------|------|
| 1 | Smallest viable audience + single hero offer | `strategy/positioning.md`, `assets/positioning.ts` |
| 2 | Kill 80% of the menu (one hero offer) | `assets/positioning.ts` (`heroOffer.hiddenServices`), `index.html` |
| 3 | Agency or product decision | `strategy/decision.md`, `assets/positioning.ts` (`decision`) |
| 4 | Park the side projects | `strategy/parked-projects.md`, `parked.html` |
| 5 | "Intensity beats extensity" brand | `assets/positioning.ts` (`brand`), `manifesto.html` |
| 6 | Permission asset (email list) | `server.js` (`/api/subscribe`), `index.html` (#join), `data/subscribers.json` |
| 7 | Story-driven landing, no feature grid | `index.html` |
| 8 | One-channel playbook | `strategy/one-channel-playbook.md`, `playbook.html` |
| 9 | Client-success seat (the missing hire) | `roles/client-success.md` |

## Layout
```
baz-refocus/
├── server.js              # static + /api/subscribe (Part 6)
├── styles.css             # BAZ brand system (navy + gold)
├── index.html             # story landing + signup (Parts 2,6,7)
├── manifesto.html         # intensity beats extensity (Part 5)
├── parked.html            # focused portfolio (Part 4)
├── playbook.html          # one-channel plan (Part 8)
├── assets/positioning.ts  # single source of truth (Parts 1,2,3,5)
├── data/subscribers.json  # permission asset store (Part 6)
├── strategy/              # positioning, decision, parked, playbook (Parts 1,3,4,8)
└── roles/client-success.md# the missing seat (Part 9)
```

input.content:
# Ogilvy × Godin — The Collaboration

Two men who rarely agree. One built the 360° brand system; the other told you to
ignore everyone but the weirdos who already get it. Applied to BAZ, they stop
fighting — because each fixes the other's blind spot.

## The tension, stated plainly
- **Godin** says: pick the smallest viable audience, be remarkable, build a tribe,
  earn permission. His weakness: "remarkable" alone doesn't pay rent — you still
  need craft, consistency, and a brand that survives contact with the world.
- **Ogilvy** says: the consumer isn't a moron, she's your wife. Craft the big idea,
  sweat every touchpoint, build a 360° brand experience. His weakness: at BAZ's
  scale, 360° everywhere is exactly the extensity Godin warns against.

## The deal they struck (for BAZ)
> **Godin chooses the ONE. Ogilvy crafts it to world-class, then wraps it in a 360°
> experience — but only for that one tribe, on that one channel, with that one offer.**

Ogilvy's discipline, Godin's scope. That's the whole collaboration.

| Question | Godin decides | Ogilvy executes |
|---|---|---|
| Who? | Smallest viable audience: technical founders | Build the brand voice *for them*, in their language |
| What? | One hero offer (45-day system) | The "big idea" + message hierarchy around it |
| Where? | One channel (email + LinkedIn) | A 360° *experience* across the few touchpoints that tribe actually hits |
| How good? | Remarkable or nothing | Craft every word, every asset to Ogilvy standard |
| Scale? | Tribe first, then a second | Only expand the 360° after the tribe compounds |

## What we take from Ogilvy (and what we reject)
From your own `bazventures-org-chart.md`: "Never copy Ogilvy's regional matrix —
irrelevant below global-enterprise scale." Agreed. So:

**Adopt from Ogilvy:**
- The *Big Idea* — one thought the whole brand hangs on ("Intensity beats extensity").
- *Craft* — "don't sell the steak, sell the sizzle"; every asset earns its place.
- The *360° touchpoint map* — but scoped to the tribe's actual journey, not the planet.
- *Positioning as a sentence* — "BAZ is the only agency that builds your whole
  marketing system in 45 days, senior-only, or you don't pay."

**Reject from Ogilvy:**
- Regional matrix, junior layers, account pyramids. (Per your org chart — keep it flat.)
- "Be everywhere." Ogilvy's 360° is for Coca-Cola. BAZ's 360° is for one tribe.

## The result
A brand that is *focused like Godin* and *crafted like Ogilvy* — the purple cow,
built by the man who taught the world to make cows people remember.

See `assets/brand-system.ts` (the Ogilvy layer) and `brand.html` (the 360° map),
`brief.html` (the creative brief). All scoped by `assets/positioning.ts` (the Godin layer).

@@ -0,0 +1,49 @@
+# Ogilvy × Godin — The Collaboration
+
+Two men who rarely agree. One built the 360° brand system; the other told you to
+ignore everyone but the weirdos who already get it. Applied to BAZ, they stop
+fighting — because each fixes the other's blind spot.
+
+## The tension, stated plainly
+- **Godin** says: pick the smallest viable audience, be remarkable, build a tribe,
+  earn permission. His weakness: "remarkable" alone doesn't pay rent — you still
+  need craft, consistency, and a brand that survives contact with the world.
+- **Ogilvy** says: the consumer isn't a moron, she's your wife. Craft the big idea,
+  sweat every touchpoint, build a 360° brand experience. His weakness: at BAZ's
+  scale, 360° everywhere is exactly the extensity Godin warns against.
+
+## The deal they struck (for BAZ)
+> **Godin chooses the ONE. Ogilvy crafts it to world-class, then wraps it in a 360°
+> experience — but only for that one tribe, on that one channel, with that one offer.**
+
+Ogilvy's discipline, Godin's scope. That's the whole collaboration.
+
+| Question | Godin decides | Ogilvy executes |
+|---|---|---|
+| Who? | Smallest viable audience: technical founders | Build the brand voice *for them*, in their language |
+| What? | One hero offer (45-day system) | The "big idea" + message hierarchy around it |
+| Where? | One channel (email + LinkedIn) | A 360° *experience* across the few touchpoints that tribe actually hits |
+| How good? | Remarkable or nothing | Craft every word, every asset to Ogilvy standard |
+| Scale? | Tribe first, then a second | Only expand the 360° after the tribe compounds |
+
+## What we take from Ogilvy (and what we reject)
+From your own `bazventures-org-chart.md`: "Never copy Ogilvy's regional matrix —
+irrelevant below global-enterprise scale." Agreed. So:
+
+**Adopt from Ogilvy:**
+- The *Big Idea* — one thought the whole brand hangs on ("Intensity beats extensity").
+- *Craft* — "don't sell the steak, sell the sizzle"; every asset earns its place.
+- The *360° touchpoint map* — but scoped to the tribe's actual journey, not the planet.
+- *Positioning as a sentence* — "BAZ is the only agency that builds your whole
+  marketing system in 45 days, senior-only, or you don't pay."
+
+**Reject from Ogilvy:**
+- Regional matrix, junior layers, account pyramids. (Per your org chart — keep it flat.)
+- "Be everywhere." Ogilvy's 360° is for Coca-Cola. BAZ's 360° is for one tribe.
+
+## The result
+A brand that is *focused like Godin* and *crafted like Ogilvy* — the purple cow,
+built by the man who taught the world to make cows people remember.
+
+See `assets/brand-system.ts` (the Ogilvy layer) and `brand.html` (the 360° map),
+`brief.html` (the creative brief). All scoped by `assets/positioning.ts` (the Godin layer).

Index: /home/uzer/baz-refocus/assets/brand-system.ts
===================================================================
--- /home/uzer/baz-refocus/assets/brand-system.ts
+++ /home/uzer/baz-refocus/assets/brand-system.ts
@@ -0,0 +1,98 @@
+// BAZ — The Ogilvy Layer.
+// Godin picks the ONE (see assets/positioning.ts). Ogilvy crafts it to
+// world-class and wraps it in a 360° experience — but ONLY for that one tribe.
+//
+// This file is the brand craft spec. It imports the Godin-scoped decisions and
+// adds: the Big Idea, the voice, the message hierarchy, and the 360° touchpoint
+// map (scoped, not planetary).
+
+import { positioning } from "./positioning";
+
+// --- The Big Idea (Ogilvy: one thought the whole brand hangs on) ---
+export const bigIdea =
+  "Intensity beats extensity — your whole marketing system, built and live in 45 days, senior-only, or you don't pay.";
+
+// --- Positioning sentence (Ogilvy: say it in one breath) ---
+export const positioningLine =
+  "BAZ is the only agency that builds your entire marketing system in 45 days with a senior-only team — or you don't pay.";
+
+// --- Voice & craft rules (Ogilvy: the consumer isn't a moron) ---
+export const voice = {
+  tone: ["plain", "confident", "specific", "a little contrarian"],
+  // Don't sell the steak, sell the sizzle — but never lie.
+  rules: [
+    "Lead with the outcome, not the feature. ('Live in 45 days' before 'we do CRM').",
+    "One idea per asset. If it has two, it has none.",
+    "Talk to a founder like a founder — no 'synergy', no 'robust solutions'.",
+    "Every number is real and defensible (45 days, $9,500, 10% of lift).",
+    "The enemy is vivid: junior-staffed agencies that send decks instead of results.",
+  ],
+  banned: ["full-service", "results-driven", "passionate about", "world-class" (unless earned), "circle back"],
+};
+
+// --- Message hierarchy (Ogilvy: what they hear first → last) ---
+export const messageHierarchy = [
+  { level: 1, msg: "Your marketing system, live in 45 days — or you don't pay." },
+  { level: 2, msg: "One senior team. No juniors, no learning curves." },
+  { level: 3, msg: "One channel, mastered — then we expand." },
+  { level: 4, msg: "Built on 'Intensity beats extensity.'" },
+];
+
+// --- 360° touchpoint map (Ogilvy), SCOPED to Godin's one tribe + one channel ---
+// Not planetary. Only the touchpoints technical founders actually hit.
+export const touchpoints360 = [
+  {
+    stage: "Awareness",
+    where: "Founder's LinkedIn feed + the weekly email essay",
+    ogilvyCraft: "One remarkable essay/week, signed by a real human, no logo spam.",
+  },
+  {
+    stage: "Consideration",
+    where: "Landing page (index.html) + manifesto",
+    ogilvyCraft: "Story before spec. The offer block is the hero, not a feature grid.",
+  },
+  {
+    stage: "Proof",
+    where: "Case line + the 45-day guarantee",
+    ogilvyCraft: "One bold promise, stated plainly, that a big agency would never risk.",
+  },
+  {
+    stage: "Conversion",
+    where: "The 'Get the 45-day plan' CTA + reply-to-essay",
+    ogilvyCraft: "One button. One next step. No menu of 14 services.",
+  },
+  {
+    stage: "Onboarding",
+    where: "First 48 hours with the senior team",
+    ogilvyCraft: "Founder meets founder. No account-manager handoff to a junior.",
+  },
+  {
+    stage: "Loyalty / tribe",
+    where: "The email list + client-success lead (roles/client-success.md)",
+    ogilvyCraft: "Renewal owned by a human, not a drip. The tribe stays a tribe.",
+  },
+];
+
+// --- What we REJECT from Ogilvy (per bazventures-org-chart.md) ---
+export const rejectedFromOgilvy = [
+  "Regional matrix — irrelevant below global-enterprise scale.",
+  "Junior layers / account pyramids — keep it flat and senior-only.",
+  "'Be everywhere' 360° — our 360° is one tribe, not the planet.",
+];
+
+export const ogilvyLayer = {
+  bigIdea,
+  positioningLine,
+  voice,
+  messageHierarchy,
+  touchpoints360,
+  rejectedFromOgilvy,
+  // Anchored to Godin's scope:
+  scopedTo: {
+    audience: positioning.audience.name,
+    offer: positioning.heroOffer.name,
+    channel: positioning.channel.primary,
+  },
+};
+
+export type OgilvyLayer = typeof ogilvyLayer;

input.content:
BAZ Offer Manifesto 360° Brand Brief Playbook Join
Ogilvy crafts it · Godin scopes it

The 360° brand — for one tribe, not the planet.

Ogilvy taught the world to make cows people remember. Godin told us to pick the one purple cow. Here, Ogilvy's craft wraps Godin's focus — a 360° experience across only the touchpoints technical founders actually hit.

The Big Idea

"Intensity beats extensity — your whole marketing system, built and live in 45 days, senior-only, or you don't pay."

One thought the whole brand hangs on. Every asset either serves it or gets cut.

Positioning, in one breath: BAZ is the only agency that builds your entire marketing system in 45 days with a senior-only team — or you don't pay.

What they hear first → last

1 · The promise

Your marketing system, live in 45 days — or you don't pay.

2 · The team

One senior team. No juniors, no learning curves.

3 · The method

One channel, mastered — then we expand.

4 · The belief

Built on "Intensity beats extensity."

The 360° touchpoint map (scoped)

Not planetary. Only the moments a technical founder actually meets BAZ.

StageWhereOgilvy craft
AwarenessFounder's LinkedIn + the weekly essayOne remarkable essay/week, signed by a human, no logo spam.
ConsiderationLanding page + manifestoStory before spec. The offer is the hero, not a feature grid.
ProofCase line + 45-day guaranteeOne bold promise a big agency would never risk.
Conversion"Get the 45-day plan" CTAOne button. One next step. No menu of 14 services.
OnboardingFirst 48 hoursFounder meets founder. No junior handoff.
LoyaltyEmail list + client-success leadRenewal owned by a human. The tribe stays a tribe.

The voice

The consumer isn't a moron. Plain, confident, specific, a little contrarian. Lead with the outcome, never the feature.

We say

  • "Live in 45 days" before "we do CRM."
  • One idea per asset — if it has two, it has none.
  • Every number is real: 45 days, $9,500, 10% of lift.

We never say

full-service · results-driven · passionate about · "world-class" (unless earned) · circle back.

BAZ — the offer · creative brief · the collab

data.text: Now the Ogilvy creative brief for the hero offer:

input.content:
BAZ Offer Manifesto 360° Brand Brief Playbook Join
The one brief · for the one offer

The creative brief: 45-day marketing system.

One offer, one audience, one channel. Ogilvy's discipline, Godin's scope. If a brief needs a second page of targets, it's broken.

The product

Your marketing system, built and running in 45 days. One senior team, one channel, one funnel.

The prospect

Technical / AI-native founders doing $20k–$250k/mo who build the product and hate junior-staffed agencies.

The promise

Live in 45 days — or you don't pay. $9,500 + 10% of attributed lift, first 90 days.

The enemy

Agencies that send decks instead of results; generalists who guess; spread-thin "be everywhere" marketing.

The single most important thing to say

"We build your entire marketing system and have it live in 45 days — senior-only, or you don't pay."

Support points (in order)

  1. One senior team — no juniors, no learning curves.
  2. One channel mastered before a second is touched.
  3. Built on "Intensity beats extensity" — focus is the strategy.

The tone

Plain, confident, specific, a little contrarian. Talk to a founder like a founder.

The media (one channel, per the playbook)

Founder-led email + LinkedIn (owned + earned). One hero essay per week. Paid only after the channel compounds.

What success looks like

calls_booked_from_list / subscribers moves. Not impressions. Not followers.

Source of truth: assets/positioning.ts (Godin) + assets/brand-system.ts (Ogilvy). Full collab logic in strategy/ogilvy-godin.md.

BAZ — back to the offer

The Ogilvy × Godin collaboration is built on top of the refocus:

**The synthesis** (`strategy/ogilvy-godin.md`): Godin picks the ONE (audience, offer, channel); Ogilvy crafts it to world-class and wraps it in a 360° experience — but only for that one tribe. We *adopt* Ogilvy's Big Idea, voice, craft, and 360° touchpoint map, and *reject* his regional matrix and "be everywhere" model (matching your own `bazventures-org-chart.md`).

**New code:**
- `assets/brand-system.ts` — the Ogilvy layer: Big Idea, positioning sentence, voice rules (with banned words), message hierarchy, and a **scoped** 360° touchpoint map. Imports Godin's `positioning.ts` so craft stays anchored to focus.
- `brand.html` — the 360° brand experience page (Ogilvy craft, Godin-scoped).
- `brief.html` — a proper Ogilvy creative brief for the 45-day hero offer.
- Nav wired in `index.html`; README updated with the new mapping.

**How the two layers connect:** `assets/positioning.ts` (Godin: who/what/where) is the single source of truth; `assets/brand-system.ts` (Ogilvy: how it's said and experienced) is scoped to it via `scopedTo: { audience, offer, channel }`. Change focus in one file, craft follows in the other.

Run it: `cd ~/baz-refocus && node server.js` → http://localhost:4173 (verified `brand.html`, `brief.html` return 200).

<content>
1: # Marketing Hub — Agency Completeness Plan
2: 
3: **Goal:** Ship every feature a professional marketing agency needs to run client work, bill accurately, and deliver a premium client experience — all inside this Next.js + SQLite hub.
4: 
5: **Architecture decision:** Client-facing portal and public intake pages stay in `marketing-hub` as public token-scoped routes. No cross-app dependency. Private workspace and public client surface share one repo, one database, one deploy. This keeps rollout atomic and avoids sync/queue complexity between `marketing-hub` and `marketing-hub-public`.
6: 
7: **Current state:**
8: - Core marketing execution is solid: SEO, ads, CRM, campaigns, email, landing pages, funnels, analytics, automations, A/B tests, inbox, surveys, reports, copy generator.
9: - Agency/finance layer is partially built: **timesheets**, **client invoices**, **project PnL**, **contractors**, **payouts**, **billing**, **portal links**, **agency client accounts**, **approvals**.
10: - Existing `clients` table (id, workspace, name, industry, website, contact_email, notes) is the canonical agency client directory. `timesheets`, `client_invoices`, and `project_pnl` already reference `client_id`.
11: - `tasks` exists but `project` is currently a TEXT string, not a FK. Add `project_id TEXT` to `tasks` so tasks can link to the new `projects` table.
12: - `/c/[token]` route and public booking pages do not exist yet; only `portal_links` token-generation API exists.
13: - What’s missing is the **client-facing experience layer** and **agency operations depth** that top platforms ship.
14: 
15: **Reuse rule:** Do not create a second client directory. All new agency tables (`proposals`, `contracts`, `projects`, `retainer_usage`, `deliverable_approvals`, `client_messages`, `client_reports`) must link to the existing `clients.id`. `agency_client_accounts` is separate and stays separate; it is for platform/sub-account billing, not agency client records.
16: 
17: **Workspace scoping:** Existing tables mix `workspace TEXT` and `workspace_id TEXT`. New tables must use `workspace_id TEXT` with FK to `workspaces.id`.
18: 
19: **Pattern to reuse everywhere:**
20: - Data: `lib/db/index.ts` auto-migration `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` inside `init()`.
21: - API: `app/api/<module>/route.ts` with GET (list), GET `[id]`, POST (create), PATCH `[id]`, DELETE `[id]`. Use existing `json/err` helpers.
22: - Pages: `app/<module>/page.tsx` and `app/<module>/[id]/page.tsx` with `"use client"`, `useFetch`, `Modal`, `apiFetch`, `lucide-react` icons, card/section layout.
23: - Nav: `components/Sidebar.tsx` group entries by department.
24: 
25: ---
26: 
27: ## Phase 1: Client Experience Layer
28: 
29: ### 1.1 White-Label Client Portal
30: **Files to create/modify:**
31: - `app/c/[token]/page.tsx` — token-scoped dashboard: project status, invoices, files, messages.
